The idea with check for list before call isinstance is an optimization for general case where doc is mostly dict or list. I agree, a common codebase would be ideal (see this comment as well https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/2876#issuecomment-385241507). However as already mentioned there are some problematic differences between the two queue implementations and its requirements which I will try to summarize below:

Heap (general_heap.pxi) is a priority queue used by watershed, Queue is used in my code:
- Multiple iterations:  Queue allows multiple iterations via `q_restore` and preserves a consumed (popped) item internally until cleared explicitly. Heap may overwrite consumed data. 
- Order: Queue uses insertion order while Heap sorts (using the array of pointers) by the return value of a function `smaller`.
- A consequence the internal data representation is different as well: Queue uses only one array to store its items while Heap uses an additional array to store pointers to its items. 

@emmanuelle You seem to be the original contributor of Heap so please point out any misunderstandings on my part.

I don't see much overlap here concerning the internal machinery of these "basic" containers. But it may be possible to implement a data container that fulfills the requirements of both Heap and Queue. But would that be wise? I think my code would be slower due to overhead introduced by the sorting machinery and watershed would have to suffer the larger memory footprint due to internal preservation of popped items.

Having said that, if you still think it might be worth it I'll try to make a quick draft of a queue that can be used as a replacement for both. That might be more useful to reach a more informed decision.

The actual problem being fixed here is that all variables declared in the "main function" (before I made it a function) become global variables and are reachable by all functions being called from it. The actual warnings from pylint was that every single variable declared didn't conform to UPPER_CASE naming style (because they're global), and that many other random functions that used the same variable names got "Redefining name <name> from outer scope".
If we want to indicate that the main function shouldn't be called I can name it something less inviting, but if we really don't want the code in a function the variables would need to be named __PRIVKEY or similar to hope that other functions don't use them.
Could move the main function into a separate file such that you'd have to explicitly import it (and place a comment in it that it's not to be imported and used) and don't automatically get it when importing discovery.py

I would prefer having `otPlatRadioFrameUpdated()` only read/write data passed in via `aFrame`.  More specifically:
1. Prepare the frame, including MAC Auxiliary Header before calling `otPlatRadioTransmit()`.
2. Radio driver updates the frame by writing the time value into the IE and calls `otPlatRadioFrameUpdated()`.
3. `otPlatRadioFrameUpdated()` performs AES-CCM by only reading/writing information in `aFrame`.

In general, OpenThread APIs should not be called in interrupt context.  `otPlatRadioFrameUpdated()` is an exception here and we should make that really clear.  In the API documentation, in addition to the note, I would indicate that `otPlatRadioFrameUpdated()` does not read/write any state within OpenThread. Let's suppose, that we are starting bubuku on several nodes (10.0.0.1, 10.0.0.2) at the same time.
After frist iteration there will be to changes in queue:
[{'start':'10.0.0.1'}, {'start':'10.0.0.2'}].
But only one of them will be applied (let's suppose that it's on 10.0.0.1). 
That means that during 'start' on 10.0.0.1 check on 10.0.0.2 will be executed several times, and each time it will say: 'broker is not started, add new change'. 
I don't want this kind of situation, so I allow only one StartChange for each instance in change queue.

I'm no fan of duplicating options between the main- and subcommands.
I would suggest to only have the `--datadir` and `--address` options on the main command. 
I.e.
The way you've build it now the usage would be:
```
raiden removedb --datadir /bla
```
However (confusingly) the following wouldn't do the correct thing yet produce no error (as in delete the default dir not the specified one):
```
raiden --datadir /bla removedb
```
And yet more confusing this would also be possible:
```
raiden --datadir /foo removedb --datadir /bla
```

From my perspective the second would the "correct" usage but I'd like to get @LefterisJP @konradkonrad and @hackaugusto opinions on this.

What's the reason of doing upgrade?

My understanding of this design is, the operator implementation store will never be cleaned up. 

One personal thinking please. I'm in favor of having the capability to clean up operator implementation store. The operator implementation store is part of onnx standard.  We may want to clean up the standard when we feel too old versions do not make sense and we want to enforce an upgrade of vendors' components (importers/exporters). Without the clean up capability, it's always up to vendors to decide the range of operator implementation store they'll support. Does this make sense please?
